findings.reachability: Any unauthenticated user can POST to admin-ajax.php with action=us_ajax_grid to trigger this handler. No authentication or nonce required.
findings.justification: The wp_ajax_nopriv_us_ajax_grid action (line 27) hooks set_search_post_ids_from_ajax() with no nonce verification and no current_user_can() check; the phpcs:ignore on line 34 confirms deliberate suppression. An unauthenticated attacker can force the server to execute a product search query with an arbitrary (sanitized) search term. State mutation is absent so severity is low, but CSRF protection is completely missing.

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF: nopriv AJAX handler without nonce
detection_pattern.description: Matches WordPress nopriv AJAX handler registrations. Files that register a nopriv handler but contain no nonce-verification call are suspicious — any visitor can trigger the handler cross-origin, potentially causing unintended server-side work or data reads.

findings.reachability: Any visitor (nopriv) or authenticated user can supply an arbitrary value in $_POST['template_vars']['query_args']['s']. Sanitization only occurs inside Helpers::searchProducts() -> getSearchResults() via sanitize_text_field() at Search.php:175.
findings.justification: The search term extracted from user-controlled JSON on line 37 ($search_term = $template_vars['query_args']['s']) is passed without any sanitization or length limit in UsCore.php itself to Helpers::searchProducts(). Safety depends entirely on the downstream sanitize_text_field() call in Search.php. If that call were removed or bypassed by a future refactor, unsanitized attacker input would reach the search engine directly. Severity is low given current downstream sanitization.

detection_pattern.description: Matches patterns where POST data is decoded from JSON without immediate sanitization in the same scope. Values extracted from such JSON and forwarded to query functions or output without a sanitization call at the call site represent a layered-defence gap.
